edges.claim: The only documented connective tissue between the UAE and Lebanon runs through the EU multilateral humanitarian channel: the UAE and the European Commission maintain a working humanitarian-logistics dialogue relevant to eastern Mediterranean crisis response, and the same Commission's 2026 Middle East humanitarian package allocates €100 million to Lebanon, positioning the UAE and the EU as parallel Gulf/European donors in the region that covers Lebanon.

GAP: No direct UAE-to-Lebanon aid package, pledge, amount, delivery operation, or ODA flow is documented anywhere in the material; Lebanon appears only as an EU humanitarian aid recipient (€100M of the EU's 2026 package), and the material explicitly states no UAE–Lebanon bilateral action is documented. Missing evidence: any UAE Aid Agency, MOFA, or FTS-tracked flow naming Lebanon as recipient.

GAP: Lebanon does not appear in the documented UAE CEPA program (in-force partners listed are India, Indonesia, Israel, Turkey, Cambodia, Georgia, Costa Rica, Mauritius, Serbia, Jordan; signed-not-in-force includes Kenya). Missing evidence: any UAE–Lebanon CEPA negotiation, trade volume figure, or trade agreement.
GAP: No ADFD, ADQ, or Mubadala deployment, loan, grant, or investment into Lebanon is documented in the material. Missing evidence: any sovereign-fund transaction, facility, or MoU naming Lebanon.

GAP: No bilateral UAE investment into Lebanon (by Mubadala or any other UAE registry entity) is documented in the material. Missing evidence: any investment announcement, FDI figure, or acquisition involving Lebanese assets.

GAP: No DP World, AD Ports, or Masdar port, logistics, energy, or infrastructure activity in Lebanon (e.g., Beirut or Tripoli port involvement) is documented in the material. Missing evidence: any concession, terminal operation, or energy project in Lebanon.

GAP: No data on the UAE–Lebanon remittance corridor (Lebanese diaspora workforce in the UAE, remittance volumes, or payment channels) is present in the material. Missing evidence: any remittance flow figure or corridor documentation.

GAP: No UAE MOFA–Lebanon diplomatic event, ministerial visit, joint statement, or strategic framework over the last two years is documented; the material explicitly notes the absence of any UAE–Lebanon diplomatic engagement. Missing evidence: any bilateral communique, embassy activity, or ministerial meeting naming Lebanon.

The UAE and the EU co-convened a panel on global humanitarian supply-chain resilience in Brussels on 11 June 2026, co-hosted by DG ECHO with more than 80 senior representatives, sustaining a working humanitarian-logistics dialogue relevant to eastern Mediterranean crisis response.

The UAE co-chaired the ministerial meeting coordinating Sudan de-escalation efforts on 24 September 2025 alongside the EU, Qatar and Kenya, and both are co-signatories of the Gaza maritime corridor ministerial joint statement.

Gaza is the largest recipient of UAE foreign aid: more than $3 billion since 2023, over 90,000 tons delivered via 8,700 trucks, 80 airdrops and 13 ships, accounting for approximately 50% of all international aid entering Gaza, with UAE-supported facilities providing medical care to 75,000 Gazans.

Ongoing 2026 UAE Gaza operations: five convoys carrying 792 tonnes (59 trucks) entered on 12 July 2026, four convoys of 47 trucks carried 416 tonnes under Operation Chivalrous Knight 3, the 301st convoy carried 290 tonnes, the 69th airdrop under Operation Birds of Goodness brought cumulative airdropped aid past 3,924 tonnes, and UAE field and floating hospitals have treated 54,000 and 24,000 patients respectively under Operation Gallant Knight 3.

The UAE announced a $30 million Sudan emergency response and pledged $500 million to a Sudan Humanitarian Fund in February 2026, and co-chaired the September 2025 ministerial meeting coordinating Sudan de-escalation.
The UAE Ministry of Foreign Affairs condemned the targeting of a WFP humanitarian convoy in Sudan and publicly supports WFP operations there.
The UAE joined the UK-anchored joint ministerial statement on advancing a maritime corridor to deliver humanitarian assistance to Gaza via a Cyprus route in the eastern Mediterranean.
The UAE and Qatar both co-signed the Gaza maritime corridor ministerial joint statement and jointly participated (with the EU and Kenya) in the Sudan de-escalation ministerial co-chair arrangement.
Cyprus and the UAE are co-signatories of the Gaza maritime corridor ministerial joint statement, with the corridor routed via Cyprus in the eastern Mediterranean.
The United States and the UAE are co-signatories of the Gaza maritime corridor ministerial joint statement.
UN OCHA's Financial Tracking Service independently tracks the UAE as donor #100, recording $94,336,382 in funded and $133,015,246 in paid UAE humanitarian flows for 2026.

The UAE President issued Federal Decree No. 27 of 2024 establishing the UAE Aid Agency as the federal instrument governing UAE regional aid; the agency operates the uaeaid.ae/opendata open-data portal and published the UAE Foreign Aid Report 2024 as the transparency framework for UAE foreign aid, which exceeds $100 billion cumulatively since 1971.

UAE humanitarian governance was further institutionalized with Sheikh Theyab bin Mohamed bin Zayed appointed chairman of the International Humanitarian and Philanthropic Council under Federal Decree No. 94 of 2026, alongside the UAE Aid Agency established by Federal Decree No. 27 of 2024.
AD Ports Group and Masdar entered a partnership in November 2025 for global offshore wind logistics.

The UAE–India CEPA (signed February 2022) drove bilateral trade to USD 100 billion in FY2024-25 with a USD 200 billion target by 2032, serving as the template for CEPA impact benchmarking; UAE non-oil foreign trade reached Dh3 trillion in 2024 with CEPAs contributing Dh135bn (+42% YoY).
The EU allocated €100 million to Lebanon within its €458 million 2026 Middle East humanitarian package announced on 16 March 2026.
Lebanon is named in European Commission coverage of Gulf-linked humanitarian and development activity, but that coverage documents no direct UAE–Lebanon bilateral action.
The EU and the UAE operate as parallel major humanitarian donors to the Middle East region covered by the €458 million 2026 package that includes Lebanon.
The EU allocated €210 million of the 2026 Middle East humanitarian package to Syria, Lebanon's neighbour, contextualising regional donor flows around Lebanon.
The UAE operates a three-mode aid delivery corridor to Gaza comprising 8,700 land trucks, 80 airdrops, and 13 maritime shipments, delivering more than 90,000 tons of aid.
The UAE and the United Kingdom are co-signatories of the joint ministerial statement on advancing a Gaza humanitarian maritime corridor via a Cyprus route in the eastern Mediterranean.
The United States and the UAE are co-signatories of the Gaza maritime corridor ministerial joint statement.
The UAE and the European Commission both joined the multinational ministerial coalition advancing a maritime corridor for humanitarian assistance to Gaza in the eastern Mediterranean.
The UAE Ministry of Foreign Affairs publicly condemned the targeting of a WFP humanitarian convoy in Sudan and supports WFP operations there.

The UAE President issued Federal Decree No. 27 of 2024 establishing the UAE Aid Agency as the federal instrument governing UAE regional aid, announced by the UAE National Media Authority on 29 June 2026.

UAE Aid, the federal foreign-aid agency, publishes official UAE foreign-aid data through its open-data portal at uaeaid.ae/opendata and issued the UAE Foreign Aid Report 2024, constituting the designated transparency mechanism for UAE aid.
UN OCHA's Financial Tracking Service independently tracks the UAE as donor #100, recording $94,336,382 in funded and $133,015,246 in paid UAE humanitarian flows for 2026.
The UAE Embassy in Washington publishes official self-reported UAE foreign-aid figures, including more than $100 billion in cumulative foreign aid since the programme began in 1971.
Sheikh Theyab bin Mohamed bin Zayed was appointed chairman of the International Humanitarian and Philanthropic Council under Federal Decree No. 94 of 2026.
